text: For a while. It takes me some time to see it, but eventually, the pattern emerges. It starts with a walk around the neighborhood, the leaves turning, a cool breeze in the air, as I take in a local park, the laughter and music washing over me in a gentle wave. Relaxing on a bench, a woman approaches me, and sits down. She is muttering to herself, her long red hair peeking out of her dirty gray sweatshirt. Her jeans have holes in the knees, and in her hands she has a bag of popcorn, which she starts to sprinkle on the ground for the pigeons. At first I pay her no attention, the day is filled with the perfume of the season—cut grass, burning leaves, and cinnamon. But underneath it is something earthy, a musty richness, emanating from her, I think, and I’m reminded of the man in the office. When the woman turns to look at me, she smiles. “It’s never that simple,” she says. “What is?” I ask. “Any of it.” She bends over, and sprinkles more popcorn, and the pigeons keep gobbling it up. One of them, a dull grey with a single splash of white on its chest stutters back and forth and tips over. It lays on the ground, twitching, and then stops moving. The woman smiles. The other birds keep eating the popcorn, some seeming to choke on it, others attacking each other, specks of blood blooming on their white feathered chests, a cacophony of screeching and wings fluttering, until in a rush of violence it all goes still, the sidewalk peppered with a dozen dead birds. The woman gets up, her bag empty, fluttering to the ground, a glint in her eye as she leans over and kisses me on the forehead. # I wander home in a daze, past the bar I used to own, which has a CLOSED sign on it, city inspection stickers all over the front door, yellow tape across it. I call the woman I’ve been dating, but a message comes on saying the number has been disconnected, or is no longer in service. I dial it again, but there is nobody there. I pass an alley, and see a familiar shape huddled by a garbage can, the man from the office. All around him is a fog of insects, flies and bees, motes and crickets. As I get closer I see that his body is contorted into an awkward shape, limbs bent, one hand lying in the gravel, each finger severed, and covered up with bandages, that seep red, with yellow rings encrusting each digit. I go back to the office. But it’s empty. I peer inside the dusty glass door, and the chairs are all there, covered in grime. There are dead plants in each corner, the walls covered in graffiti, a smell of stale cigarettes, decaying plants, and mold seeping out of a crack in the glass. I reach into my back jeans pocket looking for the business card, and find it. It says Kinko’s on it, the edges bent, the asterisk of a logo lined with pen marks, turning it into something else entirely Everything comes at a price. And I haven’t paid my fee, not even close. I have taken no responsibility. I was absent when I should have been present. I was weak when I should have been strong. I have not been absolved, because I have not sought redemption, or even asked for forgiveness. In the echo of his bang, in the silence that came after, there was an opportunity to make amends, to change my ways, and to become a better man. Instead, I did nothing. I hid in the shadows. Instead of comforting those that were left, I pushed away, defensive, and denied my guilt. And in the void that I created, the darkness pushed further outward, spiraling into her already weakening framework, pulling her down into the depth with me. And there again, I was the destroyer of worlds, an infection that continued to spread, a disease without a cure. Death is too easy. The punishment must continue. I have not learned a damn thing. So those around me suffer. I pray for guidance now, as I fall back into the darkness. But it will not be coming. Not any time soon. # A man wakes up, remembers what has happened, and screams into the void. When I first conceived of Liminal Spaces , Jessica McHugh was definitely on my list of writers I wanted to involve. After reading her story “Eye of Newt” in Campfire Macabre , I only become more convinced of it. After reading her astounding blackout poetry collection (nominated for a Bram Stoker Award) A Complex Accident of Life, there was no question about it: I had to have a Jessica McHugh story for this collection. She delivered in a big way with her wondrously gruesome and forlorn tale, “Back to One.” BACK TO ONE by Jessica McHugh T he Ninth Life Café is a gangrenous limb rotting the house to which it’s adjoined, and its owner is rotting with it. Every day her hip cat-adoption bistro remains closed due to the Covid-19 outbreak, Lisa Willis's heart withers a little more. She put up a hell of a fight to prove her small business wasn’t “non-essential,” but it was a fight she eventually lost. A lifestyle too. Not right away, of course. Pickup orders for her homemade muffins and sandwiches continued long enough to convince her she would triumph over the silly pandemic hoax. She even re-homed all but one foster cat after Ninth Life’s essentiality was called into question. But as the weeks in quarantine passed with no end in sight, pick-up orders petered out. Pop-ins became calls, calls became emails, and emails became empty platitudes like “hang in there!” in response to Facebook posts pleading for patronage. Hanging in there was no simple task.
